name: Zohar
description: The Zohar (meaning 'Splendor' or 'Radiance') is a foundational work in the literature of Jewish mystical thought known as Kabbalah. Presented as a mystical commentary on the Torah, it delves into the esoteric meanings of biblical narratives, commandments, and names of God. Proponents believe the Zohar reveals hidden dimensions of reality and offers a pathway to spiritual enlightenment. The text is written in a unique Aramaic style and is attributed to Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai, a 2nd-century CE sage, although most scholars believe it was compiled and redacted by Moses de León in 13th-century Spain. The Zohar explores themes of divine emanation (Sefirot), the nature of the soul, reincarnation (Gilgul), and the messianic era. It presents a complex cosmology where God's attributes are revealed through various emanations, shaping the physical and spiritual realms. Critics question the Zohar's historical authenticity, pointing to its late appearance and linguistic inconsistencies with 2nd-century Aramaic. Mainstream scholarship views it as a product of medieval Kabbalistic thought, reflecting the intellectual and spiritual climate of its time. While its historical claims are debated, the Zohar's profound influence on Jewish mysticism and its enduring appeal as a source of spiritual insight are undeniable. It serves as a cornerstone of Kabbalistic practice and study, offering a rich tapestry of symbolism, allegory, and philosophical speculation. The text also touches upon concepts like the Tree of Life and the significance of letters in the Hebrew alphabet, adding further layers of complexity to its esoteric teachings.
subtitle: Foundation of Jewish Mystical Thought

claims.claim: The Zohar contains ancient wisdom dating back to the 2nd century CE.
claims.evidence: Attribution to Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai and claims of oral transmission.
claims.criticism: Scholarly consensus dates its composition to the 13th century by Moses de León.

claims.claim: The Zohar reveals hidden meanings and secrets within the Torah.
claims.evidence: Complex interpretations of biblical verses and narratives, revealing deeper spiritual truths.
claims.criticism: Mainstream biblical scholarship focuses on historical and literary contexts, viewing the Zohar's interpretations as allegorical and symbolic rather than literal.

claims.claim: The Zohar provides a path to spiritual enlightenment and connection with the divine.
claims.evidence: Emphasis on mystical practices, contemplation, and understanding the divine emanations (Sefirot).
claims.criticism: Skepticism regarding the efficacy of mystical practices and the subjective nature of spiritual experiences.

keyFigures.name: Moses de León
keyFigures.role: Modern scholars believe he compiled/authored it

keyFigures.name: Gershom Scholem
keyFigures.role: Major scholar of Kabbalah who analyzed the Zohar

timeline.event: Traditional attribution to Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai
Appearance of the Zohar in Spain, attributed to Moses de León
Continued study and interpretation within Kabbalistic traditions and academic circles

sources.author: Arthur Green
sources.work: Keter: The Crown of God in Early Jewish Mysticism
sources.title: Arthur Green
